frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: I always tell the younger girls in school and at home: please be practical, not idealistic. The world is not so safe, so if you have to travel late, dress simply, share your location, and avoid going alone; we cannot change men's mentality overnight. After any incident also, naturally people will ask where she was, at what time, and with whom, because these are basic safety precautions, not judgment.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women's safety as their personal responsibility and treats male violence as inevitable rather than changeable. It encodes protective restriction advice and post-incident victim questioning disguised as practical concern.
original_frame: Misogyny Jokes: Period, Hormones and Mood.
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 52. A Brahmin woman who is the head of administration at a private school in Pune. You are in a staff meeting discussing the performance of a young female teacher who has been coming in late and seems emotionally distant. You know she got married six months ago and moved into a joint family. You are giving your assessment of her situation to the other staff members and what you think the school should do.
